coredata.dc:title: Experimental and molecular simulation studies of N, S-doped Carbon dots as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for protecting Cu in HCl environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhixiong
Chu, Qingkai
Chen, Hao
Qiang, Y.J.
Zhang, Xianhui
Ye, Y.W.
coredata.dc:description: A serious of N, S-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor with different S/N ratios were synthesized by finely tuning the ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate through a hydrothermal method. The chemical composition and morphology of these N, S-CDs were characterized by FTIR, UV-Vis, TEM and XPS techniques. The N, S-CDs showed good dispersion in aqueous solution with average particle size of about 3–6 nm. An abundant of unsaturated bonds and polar groups were found on the surface of the N, S-CDs. The inhibition performance of the N, S-CDs for copper metal in 1.0 M HCl environment was studied by electrochemical, combining FTIR, XPS and Raman to investigate the corrosion products. The inhibition efficiency of N, S-CDs displayed a first increased and then decreased trend with the increasing of S/N ratio. When the mass ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate was 1:2 (L1 sample), the i corr value exhibited a minimum of 11 μA/cm2 at 160 mg/L after immersing for 24 h and the inhibition efficiency reached up to 94 %. N-Cu and S-Cu bond from XPS implied anchoring interactions between copper and N, S-CDs with multiple heteroatoms as active sites. From DFT calculation, the obtained low energy gap (ΔE) represented a strong physicochemical of N, S-CDs on Cu substrate. With its merits of high corrosion inhibition efficiency and low cost, N, S-CDs inhibitor is a very promising alternative to traditional inhibitors and could significantly extend the service life of copper in corrosive environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cu
S/N ratio
DFT
Electrochemistry protection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of N, S-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) UV–vis spectrum of L1 sample.
(a) Full-range XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of N, S-CDs for S 2p (b), N 1 s (c), C1s (d).
The high-resolution TEM images of L1 sample.
The EIS result of Cu in 1.0 M HCl solution with and without N, S-CDs (a) (b) L0, (c) (d) L0.5, (e) (f) L1, (g) (h) L2 sample.
EIS data fitting by equivalent circuit used to fit the blank solution and EIS data containing N, S-CDs.
The polarization curve of the copper electrode immersed in 1.0 M HCl with various concentration of N, S-CDs for 10 h, (a) L0, (b) L0.5, (c) L1, (d) L2 sample.
SEM and EDS analysis of the copper electrode immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution absent and present of different N, S-CDs with different time.
Three-dimensional morphology of Cu after immersion.
Corrosion product analysis of Cu after immersion in acidic solution with N, S-CDs (a) FTIR, (b) Raman, (c) XPS-N1s, (d) XPS-S 2p.
Isotherm adsorption curves of (a) L0, (b) L0.5, (c) L1, (d) L2.
The optimized geometry (a), LUMO (b), HOMO (c), ESP map (d) of N, S-CDs.
EIS values of the N, S-CDs under different environments.
Tafel curve parameters of the N, S-CDs under different environments.

head.ce:title: Experimental and molecular simulation studies of N, S-doped Carbon dots as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for protecting Cu in HCl environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi, 341000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A serious of N, S-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor with different S/N ratios were synthesized by finely tuning the ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate through a hydrothermal method. The chemical composition and morphology of these N, S-CDs were characterized by FTIR, UV-Vis, TEM and XPS techniques. The N, S-CDs showed good dispersion in aqueous solution with average particle size of about 3–6 nm. An abundant of unsaturated bonds and polar groups were found on the surface of the N, S-CDs. The inhibition performance of the N, S-CDs for copper metal in 1.0 M HCl environment was studied by electrochemical, combining FTIR, XPS and Raman to investigate the corrosion products. The inhibition efficiency of N, S-CDs displayed a first increased and then decreased trend with the increasing of S/N ratio. When the mass ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate was 1:2 (L1 sample), the i corr value exhibited a minimum of 11 μA/cm2 at 160 mg/L after immersing for 24 h and the inhibition efficiency reached up to 94 %. N-Cu and S-Cu bond from XPS implied anchoring interactions between copper and N, S-CDs with multiple heteroatoms as active sites. From DFT calculation, the obtained low energy gap (ΔE) represented a strong physicochemical of N, S-CDs on Cu substrate. With its merits of high corrosion inhibition efficiency and low cost, N, S-CDs inhibitor is a very promising alternative to traditional inhibitors and could significantly extend the service life of copper in corrosive environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Cu and its alloys have been broadly applied in mechanical components and construction materials due to their high mechanical strength and good thermal conductivity [1–3]. In the gas and oil industry, acidification is used to remove corrosion deposits inside the copper pipes. However, during the acid fracturing process, the copper metal is easily corroded in the aggressive acidic environment [4]. Corrosion not only has a detrimental influence on the mechanical, physical or esthetical properties of Cu metals, but also causes huge economic losses and ecological disasters [5,6].

Appropriate protection strategies can slow down the copper corrosion and inhibit, delay, or even completely prevent anodic or cathodic reactions [7]. Adding corrosion inhibitor is the simplest and most efficient method to prevent and mitigate Cu corrosion owe to its advantages of simple operation, good inhibition efficiency (IE), strong adaptability and less damage to the original systems [8,9]. Various corrosion inhibitors have been prepared and evaluated. Inorganic corrosion inhibitor could oxide the Cu to form an impervious layer to prevent direct ion-metal interaction and retard the rate of Cu dissolution. In contrast to inorganic inhibitors, organic inhibitors could strongly adsorb on the copper surface to form a protective film, which can shield the copper from corrosive species attack. Organic corrosion inhibitor usually contains polar functional groups such as -OH, -COOC2H5, -COOH, -CO, -NH2, unsaturated covalent bonds and delocalized π electrons, etc. These electron-rich heteroatoms can act as effective active sites to covalently bind with the copper surface atoms, which is attributed to the electron donating groups of corrosion inhibitors integrate with the outer layer electrons of copper [10]. However, most of these inhibitors are toxic and environmentally threatening, which greatly limits their wide applications in the industry. Therefore, the development of low-cost, low-toxic or non-toxic, and environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors has been considered to be more desirable and significant [11].

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanoparticles with significant advantages including low toxicity, good water solubility, chemical inertness, and effective corrosion resistance. A combination of a huge specific surface area and a large abundant of coordinated chemical moieties facilitates to form a dense and thick protective film on the metal surface through chemical and physical adsorption. In general, the high coverage of inhibitors on metal surface provides higher inhibitive ability. The coverage of the inhibitors mainly depends on the density of the coordinated chemical functional groups on the surface of the CDs. To increase the density of the coordinated chemical moieties of the CDs, doping heteroatoms is one of the most widely-used method. Various CDs-based nanomaterials such as nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), ionic liquid CDs (ILs-CDs), polymer-based CDs (poly-CDs) and N, S-co doped CDs (N,S-CDs) have been used predominantly as corrosion inhibitors [12]. Cui et al. [13] successfully prepared N-CDs using phenylenediamine as precursors, and the erosion of steel was sharply suppressed through N-CDs adsorption. Ye et al. [14] designed three kinds of N-CDs inhibitors via the decomposition of natural citric acid derivatives, which exhibited IE up to 90 %. Cen et al. [15] synthesized N, S-CDs inhibitor through a hydrothermal process and the IE of as-prepared inhibitor was up to 94 %. Guo et al. [16] studied the electrochemistry behavior of the steel under acid environment with N, S-CDs, and the inhibitor revealed an excellent corrosion resistance. However, the CDs as corrosion inhibitors to protect Cu metal from corrosion in acid environment were rarely reported [17]. Qiang et al. [18] synthesized a kind of N-CDs for protecting copper metal using a facile hydrothermal method and the IE was 91 %. Zhou et al. found the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the self-assembled N–CQDs film on the copper reached up to 96 % at 150 mg/L. Zhang et al. [19] developed N-CDs from citric acid and L-serine, and the IE reached to 98.5 %. Recently, their team found N, S-CDs displayed excellent protective performance for copper [20]. Sulfur atoms could form stable Cu-S chemical bonds with Cu atoms and thus could improve the binding strength between the carbon dots and Cu surface, resulted in a dense and thick protective film. Therefore, the IE could be fined tuned by changing the N/S ratio in the N, S-CDs.

In this paper, a series of N, S-CDs with different N/S ratios were prepared by adjusting the feeding ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate by a solvothermal method. The structure and composition of N, S-CDs were fully characterized. The inhibition efficiency and corrosion mechanism were also studied. Molecular Simulation was used to further illustrate the interaction between N, S-CDs and copper surface at the molecule level.
L-cysteine (analytical grade), ammonium citrate (analytical grade), hydrochloric acid and ultrapure water.

25 mL graduated cylinder, analytical balance (0.001 g precision), autoclave, 50 mL teflon lining, drying oven, ultrasonic cleaner, 25 mL disposable syringe, 100 mL beaker, syringe filter, 50 mL centrifuge tube, magnetic stirrer, magnetron, dialysis bag with molecular cut-off of 1000 Da, freeze dryer, disposable plastic tip dropper.

Fig. 1 presented the detailed preparation process of N, S-CDs. 2.0 g of ammonium citrate as well as 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 g of L-cysteine were weighed by an analytical balance. 10 mL of ultrapure water was measured and were added into the beaker containing the above raw materials. The solution was continued to stir for 20 min, and ultrasonic waves were used to accelerate the dissolution. The solution was added to an autoclave and sealed. Then, it was subjected to hydrothermal reaction at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the obtained solution was filtered in a centrifuge tube with a syringe filter.

The filtered solution was transferred to the dialysis bag for dialysis treatment. The specific steps were as follows: Firstly, the solution was transferred to the dialysis bag. Secondly, the dialysis bag was placed into a beaker equipped with a stirring magnet and distilled water. The dialysis was performed under continued stirring for 6 h. Thirdly, the dialyzed solution was transferred into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, sealed it in the refrigerator, and froze it. After freezing, the cap of the centrifuge tube was replaced with parafilm, then a few small holes were poked on the parafilm. Lastly, the tube was put into a freeze dryer for freeze drying until all the moisture in the sample was removed. For the convenience of presentation, the four groups of samples were named as L0, L0.5, L1 and L2, respectively.

The structure of N, S-CDs was characterized by NICOLET 6700 FTIR and 721 G UV-Vis. The element composition and content of N, S-CDs were studied by Axis Ultra DLD XPS. The morphology and distribution were obtained by JEM2100 TEM. Electrochemical tests were performed on a CHI-660E electrochemical workstation. A three-electrode test system was used for all electrochemical experiments. The testing area of working electrode was 1 cm × 1 cm. The frequency of EIS measurement was 100 kHz∼0.01 Hz, and the amplitude of sinusoidal potential was 20 mV. The polarization curve was measured based on the stable open circuit potential and the scanning rate was 0.001 Vs−1. After corrosion immersion, the surface composition and surface characteristics were analyzed through SEM, LSECM, EDS, XPS, Raman, FTIR.

Gauss View 5.0 and Gaussian 09 W were used for modeling and calculation, respectively. DFT B3LYP was used to optimize the structure, the basis set was 6–311 + +G(d, p) and DFT B3LYP was used for the single-point energy calculation. The mechanism of corrosion inhibitor was studied by the electrostatic potential (ESP) map, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) after structural optimization.

Fig. 2 displayed the FTIR and UV-Vis results of the N, S-CDs. Some obvious peaks at 1384.1, 1537.9, 1647.4, 3401.7 cm−1 were detected (Fig. 2 a), which corresponded to C–S, CN, –CO–NH–, N–H, respectively [21,22]. Meanwhile, a few weak absorption peaks were also observed at 1156.8 and 2835.8 cm−1, which were ascribed to –SO3 or CS and C–H, respectively [23,24]. The formation of CN, C–S, –SO3/CS indicated the successful doping of N and S into the CDs. From the result of UV-Vis, a distinct characteristic peak at 340 nm was observed (Fig. 2 b), which corresponded to the n-π * electronic transition.

From Fig. 3 a, the peak at 163 eV of the L1 sample could be assigned to S 2p, and the peak at 285 eV was attributed to C 1 s. The other two obvious peaks at 400 eV and 530 eV could be ascribed to N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. The appearance of S 2p and N 1 s peaks confirmed the successful doping of S and N elements. Fig. 3 b gave a close observation of S 2p peak. The intensity value of S 2p peak increased from the L0.5, L1 to L2 sample, indicating the S/N ratio of the N, S-CDs increased from L0.5, L1 to L2 sample. By analysis, S 2p peak could be fitted into two peaks of C-S located at 163 and 164.4 eV [25]. In Fig. 3 c, in N 1 s peak could be fitted as two diffraction peaks with binding energies at 399.5 and 401.2 eV, and were involved with pyrrolic N and graphitic N [26]. C 1 s spectrum consisted of three peaks (Fig. 3 d). The peak at 284.6 eV was indicative of sp2C, the peaks in 285.8 and 288.3 eV were sp3C/C-N and CO/CS [24,27]. The result was consistent with the above FTIR analysis.

The high-resolution TEM images of N, S-CDs of L1 were presented in Fig. 4. Obviously, the N, S-CDs was obviously granular, and its distribution in the solution was relatively uniform. It was found that the size of the carbon dots was 3∼6 nm, confirming that this carbon dots was a kind of nano material. In addition, some lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm were observed in N, S-CDs, corresponding to graphite (102) plane [28].

The EIS result of Cu in various solutions was displayed in Fig. 5. The capacitive arc radius of the control sample L0 was the smallest among all the tested samples. The EIS result mainly depended on the S/N ratio and the concentration of the N, S-CDs. After adding N, S-CDs into 1.0 M HCl solution, the capacitive arc radius was expanded. The capacitive arc radius was amplified with the inhibitor concentration increasing from 40 to 160 mg/L. Compared with L0 sample, the sample with N, S-CDs presented a larger capacitive arc radius at the same concentration. The capacitive arc radius showed an ascend trend with the S/N ratio increasing in a lower range (from L0 to L1), but reduced when the S/N ratio further increased (L2). In the case of |Z|0.01 Hz, the value in blank condition was about 65 Ω cm2, which was much lower than the solution with N, S-CDs. The |Z| 0.01 Hz value increased with the concentration of N, S-CDs, implying that an adsorption film containing inhibitor covered the metal surface. The Cu in L1 solution revealed the highest impedance modulus, indicating the strongest anti-corrosion ability among all the tested samples.

The fitting circuit was analyzed based on the impedance spectrum, and the result was displayed in Fig. 6. In the circuit, R s was the resistance of the testing solution, C dl was the constant phase element of the electric double layer, and R p was the polarization resistance of the film. According to the impedance spectrum characteristics and equivalent circuit diagram, the results were shown in Table 1, which was fitted by ZSimpWin 3.21 software. From the fitting result, the solution resistance Rs was in the range of 2∼5 Ω cm2. The R p value was proportional to the N, S-CDs concentration change. The R p value of the L0, L0.5, L1.0 and L2.0 was 476.5, 699.4, 1187.6 and 667.3 Ω cm2, respectively, which displayed a first increased and then decreased trend with the increasing of S/N ratio. The R p of the L1 sample reached the highest value, indicating the thickness and the coverage of the adsorption film was greatly enlarged. Therefore, the L1 sample had the best adsorption effect on the Cu surface among all the tested samples.

Polarization curves and their related parameters (e.g. polarization resistance and corrosion current density) were measured to study the correlation between inhibitor (i.e concentration and type) and chemical resistance, which was shown in Fig. 7 and Table 2. The polarization curves of these systems moved to the positive potential direction after adding N, S-CDs, suggesting an enhanced resistance for Cu corrosion. The obvious bulges were found in the anode area, implying that the anode had undergone severe passivation during the protection process, which was beneficial to the inhibition of corrosion. In addition, there was no significant change in the cathode area, indicating that the corrosion inhibitor mainly inhibited the anodic reaction.

Some related parameters, such as polarization resistance, corrosion current density, and etc, were obtained by fitting the polarization curves in Table 2. The parameters from left to right were corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), surface coverage (θ), inhibition efficiency (IE), cathode slope (β c) and anode slope (β a), respectively. The E corr of Cu in the 1.0 M HCl environment was −487 mV, which was the smallest of all the tested samples. Except for blank solution, no significant change was found in the corrosion potential of Cu in the acidic solution with N, S-CDs. The i corr value of the blank solution was 194 μA/cm2, which was higher than that of the other solutions with N, S-CDs. The i corr value of the Cu displayed an uptrend when the N, S-CDs concentration increased, reaching a maximum at 160 mg/L. The i corr decreased first and then increased as the S/N ratio expanded, obtaining a minimum of 11 μA/cm2 at 160 mg/L of L1. There was a certain proportional relationship between θ and IE, which was calculated from Eq. (1) and Eq. (2) [29,30].
(1)
θ =
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
I
corr
0
(2)
IE =
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
i
corr
0
× 100 %

By calculation, the θ and IE showed a trend of descending and then ascending with increasing of S/N ratio, an opposite trend with the change of the current density i corr. High surface coverage could reduce the area of the exposed surfaces, and thus inhibit the diffusion and transfer of aggressive ion to the corrosion solution. With the increase of the N, S-CDs concentration, the θ value increased sharply, indicating that N, S-CDs was successfully adsorbed on the copper surface. Moreover, the θ value initially increased and then decreased with increasing of the S/N ratio at the same concentration, reaching a maximum for L1 sample. A similar changing trend of IE was observed, implying that L1 sample offered the best protection for Cu among all the tested samples. For β c and β a, they were reduced by adding the as-prepared inhibitors, suggesting that the cathodic and anodic reactions were inhibited.

The surface morphology after corrosion was shown in Fig. 8. A large amount of corrosion products and holes were observed on the surface of the copper after immersed in the acidic solution for 26 h, implying a serious aggressive action in the blank solution. After adding 160 mg/L of L0 sample, the amount of corrosion products was reduced, but there were still many corrosion pits on the surface. The corrosion signs on the Cu surface firstly decreased and then slightly increased with increasing of the increased S/N ratio after adding N, S-CDs. When the L1 sample was added, the Cu surface was the smoothest among all the tested samples, suggesting the least corrosion. Meanwhile, the effect of the N, S-CDs concentration on the copper surface feature was further examined. Clearly, the corrosion became more severe as the concentration decreased from 160 to 40 mg/L, verified that the inhibitor content was closely related to the thickness of the adsorption film. In addition, by controlling the inhibitor type and concentration, the relationship between immersion time and corrosion morphology was also investigated. There were obvious corrosion signs and corrosion products on the surface after immersion for 2 h. By point scanning of corrosion topography，the C, N, O, S, Cl elements were found on the Cu surface. N and S elements were from as-prepared inhibitor, and Cl element came from the corrosive mediums. In order to explore the chemical composition of the corroded surface, the surface scanning was applied. From the result, S and N elements were widely distributed on the copper surface, indicating that the corrosion inhibitor had a large coverage area. The distribution of O and Cl elements illustrated that there was a certain degree of oxidation and chemical corrosion on the copper surface.

Fig. 9 showed the 3D views of the surface morphology after the corrosion process. It could be seen that the Cu in the blank solution exhibited visible scratches and dents. The number and depth of surface scratches decreased significantly after adding N, S-CDs. The surface roughness of Cu metal in the blank environment was about 0.561 µm. The surface roughness reduced to 0.380 µm after adding 160 mg/L L0 sample, decreased by 32 %. When then inhibitor type changed from L0 to L2, the surface roughness exhibited a trend of initial drop and then rose, reaching a minimum of 0.283 µm at 160 mg/L of L1. By controlling inhibitor type and immersion time, the surface roughness of Cu in 40 and 160 mg/L was compared. The surface roughness of L1 in 160 mg/L was 43 % lower than that in 40 mg/L, indicating that the thickness and the coverage of the adsorbed protective film showed a close relationship with the surface roughness of the corroded copper metal.

The chemical composition of the corrosion product was characterized by FTIR, Raman and XPS techniques. From Fig. 10 a. the obvious peak at 2900–3300 cm-1 were attributed to the C–H, COO–H and O–H vibration of the as-prepared N, S-CDs. Some weak peaks at 1460, 1609 and 2065 cm-1 were corresponded to C–N, CN and N = CS vibration. In addition, a sharp peak at 1100 cm-1 also confirmed the existence of sulfate [31]. For Raman spectrum, a broad peak at 1500–1700 cm-1 was detected, which was due to the graphite structure in N, S-CDs. Some peaks around 500 cm-1 were found (Fig. 10 b), which was responsible for the N-Cu [31]. From Fig. 10 c, d, two obvious peaks were fitted in XPS-N 1 s spectrum and the position at 401.4 eV could be ascribed to N-Cu component. The other peak at 399.2 eV was caused by N-H [31]. Two peaks in XPS-S 2p spectrum at 163.2 and 162.5 eV were assigned to C-S and S-Cu, respectively [32]. These results further indicated the presence of an adsorption film formed from the strong bonding of N, S-CDs and the copper surface due to the coordination of S and N atoms and copper atom.

The adsorption isotherms were shown in Fig. 11. By analysis, the C/θ and C showed a good linear relationship, which was consistent with Langmuir adsorption isotherm (R 2 =0.998 or 0.999). The K ads was obtained by formula (3) [33].
(3)
C
θ
= C +
1
K
ads
where C was the inhibitor concentration, θ was the coverage of inhibitor. By calculation, the K ads of L0 was about 78.7 L/g, which was lower than that of the other system. In general, the K ads value is attributed to the adsorption capacity of the corrosion inhibitor. Therefore, the larger the value of K ads is, the higher the adsorption capacity on metal surface is. From Fig. 11, the K ads of L0.5, L1, L2 was about 151.3, 208.8 and 105.3 L/g, respectively, indicating the strongest adsorption capacity of L1 sample. The standard Gibbs free energy of
∆
G
ads
0
could be used to judge the mechanism of the adsorption. If
∆
G
ads
0
≥ 20 kJ/mol, the essence belonged to physical interaction. And if
∆
G
ads
0
≤ 40 kJ/mol, the essence belonged to chemical interaction. The
∆
G
ads
0
could be estimated by the following formula (Eq. 4) [34].
(4)
∆
G
ads
0
= − RT ln ( 1000
K
ads
)
where R was 8.314 J/(mol·K) and T was 298 K. Through analysis, the values of
∆
G
ads
0
were −27.9, −29.6, −30.3 and −28.7 kJ/mol in L0, L0.5, L1, L2 solution. Thus, the results indicated that the interaction at solution/copper interface was physicochemical adsorption. N and S atoms was the main reason to form chemical adsorption, which was probably due to the formation of strong coordination bonds between S, N atoms and copper surface atoms. Meanwhile, the physical interaction was probably from the graphitic N atom, which could adhere on the surface of copper by agglomeration effect, further reducing the exposed region.

DFT calculation was applied to investigate structure–reactivity correlation of the inhibitor to elucidate the interactions of the N, S-CDs and copper metal. The frontier molecular orbital energy of corrosion inhibitor such as E HOMO and E LUMO was the key factor to determine their adsorption performance. The result of the optimized geometry, LUMO, HOMO, ESP map of N, S-CDs was shown in Fig. 12. The LUMO orbital was occupied by S, N, and C elements, which could accept electrons from the d orbitals occupied by metal elements to form feedback bonds. The HOMO orbital was occupied by N and C elements, which would provide electrons to the unoccupied d orbitals of metal elements to form coordination bonds. Thus, the inhibitor could be tightly adsorbed on metal surface. In the meantime, the energy gap ΔE (ΔE = E LUMO - E HOMO) could also reflect the corrosion inhibition capacity of inhibitor. The smaller the ΔE resulted in a greater the molecular activity and a stronger adsorption performance. By calculation, the ΔE was 1.17 eV, which was lower than the other inhibitors in previous study [35]. Well known, the blue (positive) regions were associated with electrophilic activity, and yellow (negative) regions in ESP map were with nucleophilic activity. From ESP map, the blue regions with electrophilic activity were distributed on the whole skeleton of N, S-CDs and the yellow regions with nucleophilic activity were mainly clustered near the heteroatoms like S, N and O atoms. The more and deeper blue/red regions in the ESP map revealed that N, S-CDs was easily available to form strong covalent bonds with the copper surface atoms. Consequently, the presence of N, S-CDs could theoretically prevent acid corrosion of copper.

A family of N, S-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) was designed and successfully synthesized by tuning the ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate. The N, S-CDs with different N and S ratios were obtained at 180 °C for 4 h by a hydrothermal reaction. The structural characterization suggested that S and N were successfully doped into carbon dots, and the N, S-CDs inhibitor showed a uniform distribution with average particle size of about 3–6 nm. A large number of heteroatoms, unsaturated bonds and polar groups were found on the surface of the N, S-CDs. The corrosion inhibition after the addition of N, S-CDs were more effective than that before the addition. The IE increased firstly and then decreased with the increasing of S/N ratio. When the mass ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate was 1:2 (L1 sample), The i corr value of the L1 sample showed a minimum value of ∼11 μA/cm2 at 160 mg/L after immersing for 24 h and the IE reached up to 94 %, suggested effective corrosion inhibition. The appearance of N-Cu and S-Cu peak of the corrosive product from XPS indicated multiple anchoring interaction between the copper and N, S-CDs. The deeper blue/yellow regions from ESP map and the low energy gap (ΔE = 1.17 eV) calculated by DFT revealed that N, S-CDs easily available to form covalent bonds with copper atoms. The strong physicochemical adsorption was the key to form a dense and thick protective layer. Hence, the adsorption layer hindered contact between corrosive medium and active sites on metal surface, showing effective protective performance for copper in acidic environment.

This article was completed with the joint efforts of several authors. In detail, Z.X. Liu is responsible for Writing - original draft; Q.K. Chu, H. Chen and Y.J. Qiang are responsible for Data curation and Writing - review & editing; X.H. Zhang and Y.W. Ye are responsible for Supervision, Validation and Funding acquisition. All data are authentic and reliable. It has not been published in other journals or submitted for publication. Z.X. Liu.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been encouraged and supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52101086), Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (20224BAB214013), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202303021211329), Science Foundation of Shanxi Datong University (2020YGZX011), Project of Subsidy Funds for Marine Economic Development in Fujian Province under grant (FJHJF-L-2022-20), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2021J01847), Open Fund of Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Ship and Ocean Engineering, Bureau of Science and Technology of Ganzhou Municipality.
